HI @tigran-a, you're looking for the `reset_offset_on_start` and `auto_offset_reset` [kwargs](http://pykafka.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/balancedconsumer.html):

```python
from pykafka.common import OffsetType
consumer  = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
    consumer_group="B",
    zookeeper_connect="...",
    consumer_timeout_ms=100,
    auto_commit_enable=True,
    reset_offset_on_start=True,
    auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.LATEST)
```
This combination of arguments will cause the consumer to start consuming from the latest available offset.

I replicated your setup and I think I have an idea of what's going on here. It looks like the issue is indeed an integer-to-string conversion, but it's happening with this block of code in `WordPrinter`:

```python
        word0 = tup.values[0]
        word1 = tup.values[1]
        self.log('in tryme: ' + word0 + ', ' + word1)
```

In the Clojure file, the input source of `tryme-bolt` is configured to be the output of `count-bolt`, so `tup.values[1]` is an `int` (and thus, so is `word1`). Concatenating `word1` with a string causes Python to raise an exception. However, the message is not logged but instead output to `stderr` (on the VM), so it doesn't get back to the streamparse process.

Updating the `process` function to catch and print the exception will cause the message to log:

```python
    def process(self, tup):

        word0 = tup.values[0]
        word1 = tup.values[1]
        try:
            self.log('in tryme: ' + word0 + ', ' + word1)
        except TypeError as e:
            self.log('\nlooks like a TypeError: %s' % str(e))
```

The fix, in this case, is to cast `word1`, or to just change the log statement to `self.log('in tryme: %s, %d' % (word0, word1))`. 

Casting the value in `WordCounter` fixes your issue because now `WordPrinter` is getting two strings, but the root cause is a Python type casting error, not that you can't emit non-strings.

Hope this helps!

@matt-gardner  Thanks, You mentioned earlier about beaker internally here (#1476). I am assuming that it takes care of scheduling / running / evaluating experiments and generating nice analysable reports. I believe that in simple case, this roughly ties together with **Part B** and **Part C** as I mentioned in above comment. But that's not what this PR proposes.

I doubt if a general purpose separate tool with those use cases can generate sub experiments like this command. Generating valid experiments configs (for allennlp) is very much tied together with specifics of allennlp (eg. valid jsonnet) and specifics of your own model / experiment (eg. input of classifier layer should be doubled if I use bidirectional lstm and such idiosyncrasies).

One can surely have a script (specific to his/her experiment) to generate different `--overrides` and have different shell commands which can scheduled/run/evaluated and analysed by whatever tool. But `--overrides` can become very cumbersome even for simple example that I give above. Having different sub experiment config files is much more managable option than managing different `--overrides`.  Alternatively, one can manually generate sub-experiment configs files manually - but is error prone when one wants to explore more than few changes or combinations. 

So independent of what tool one uses for scheduling / running / analysing etc, an easy to use tool for generating valid sub-experiments configs for allennlp (based on what a researcher wants to explore) would be a helpful add-on.

If so, I'm guessing from_email is more complicated than the example you gave, because that specific address should work fine:

```python
>>> from anymail.utils import parse_address_list
>>> from_email = parse_address_list(['First Last <firstlast@outlook.com>'])[0]
>>> str(from_email)
'First Last <firstlast@outlook.com>'
>>> from_email.addr_spec
u'firstlast@outlook.com'
```

For Apache this sadly isn't very straightforward;

`SSLOpenSSLConfCmd` is available only in Apache > 2.4.8, and specifying dhparams there will also require OpenSSL > 1.0.2. 

The alternative way to do this is to append dhparams to the certificate file. This requires Apache > 2.4.7 and OpenSSL > 0.9.8a to work.

Apache and OpenSSL versions and possible approach in few major binary distros is as follows:

## Debian
### Wheezy - APPEND
Apache 2.2.22-13+deb7u11
OpenSSL 1.0.1t-1+deb7u2
### Jessie - APPEND
Apache 2.4.10-10+deb8u10
OpenSSL 1.0.1t-1+deb8u6
### Stretch - CONFIG
Apache 2.4.25-3+deb9u2
OpenSSL 1.1.0f-3

## Ubuntu
### Trusty - APPEND
Apache 2.4.7-1ubuntu4.17
OpenSSL 1.0.1f-1ubuntu2.22
### Xenial - CONFIG
Apache 2.4.18-2ubuntu3.4
OpenSSL 1.0.2g-1ubuntu4.6
### Zesty - CONFIG
Apache 2.4.25-3ubuntu2.2
OpenSSL 1.0.2g-1ubuntu11

## CentOS
### 6.9 - APPEND
Apache 2.2.15
OpenSSL 1.0.1e
### 7-1611 - APPEND
Apache 2.4.6
OpenSSL 1.0.1e

## OpenSuSE
### 42.1 - APPEND
Apache 2.4.16
OpenSSL 1.0.1
### 42.2 - CONFIG
Apache 2.4.23
OpenSSL 1.0.2j
### 42.3 - CONFIG
Apache 2.4.23
OpenSSL 1.0.2j
### tumbleweed - CONFIG
Apache 2.4.27
OpenSSL 1.1.0

While long requested, the Elasticsearch team has pretty much _always_ recommended that people _not_ use index size as a determining characteristic.  In fact, while it's absolutely possible to have a 200G shard size, we typically recommend that users not let shard sizes grow past 50G, and even 20G-30G as a best-practices maximum.  These recommendations are primarily because we know too well how long it takes for data to migrate between nodes.

I will contemplate adding this feature, but please know if I don't it's mostly because I worry that if Curator supports it, it gives the appearance that Elastic and the Elasticsearch developers put the stamp of approval on cluster practices it doesn't actually recommend.

I agree with too many shards is not good.   This is also part of the reason I am doing cleanup on this particular cluster.   I've migrated the large indices off and keeping the max number of shards to 8+0 for the larger indices, the rest are kept at 3+0.    This cluster has been stable and working fine for several months now.  Even with the larger indices on it.   I am moving them off to help distribute the load and increase performance.

With all that said...   Instead of reporting deletion OK, perhaps curator can say that deletion has been queued OK, but to check later to confirm.   That would seem to be more accurate.   All of my maintenance are done via scripts, but if my scripts can not trust the results curator is returning, it would be a problem.

It explains why some of my clusters are running out of disk space even with maintenance script running nightly.   I have now added in a loop to run script 10 times or until curator report there is no matching indices to ensure that the indices are deleted.

> The 'do something' message, like the 'load this resource' message comes from the kernel. Hence, if the message is request/response, 'load this resource' function in the kernel would return a defered (or something, whatever is best for the language), in which, once it's resolved would send the 'do something' message.

But to implement that, you need the frontend to send a receipt right back to the kernel to acknowledge that the resource has been received. It's not enough for the kernel to know that it *sent* the resource, it has to wait until the frontend has received it. And then it needs to think about what to do if it doesn't get that receipt within a timeout, and so on.

It really seems like it would be much simpler to have the frontend *request* resources when it's trying to do something that requires them. That's already the way HTTP+HTML works anyway, so it should be easier to implement.

IIUC, kernels only get culled if all of the following conditions are satisfied ([code](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/43a97807fcdf04bd66968593bf954c4201c2b696/notebook/services/kernels/kernelmanager.py#L412)): 

* they've been idle for longer than the timeout
* they're not currently busy
* they don't have any active connections

As long as you have your browser open to a notebook, the notebook app will keep the kernel running, even if the kernel is idle for an hour.

mozdownload depends on mozinfo, the the tests depend on mozfile and mozprocess. These packages belong to mozbase, which does not yet support Python 3. Furthermore, these packages are not used or testing in Python 3 environments. We would either need to explore alternatives, or mark this issue as dependent on one for mozbase to be upgraded to support Python 3. If you are interested, this would be a very valuable contribution, however I think the first part to take on would be to create an environment where we would test these packages using Python 3. Without this, there would be no guarantee that support for Python 3 wouldn't regress. It looks like this was [raised 2 years ago](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1093212), but little work has been done.

@chetan51 Thanks for the review. I have addressed your comments.

I don't think it's a bug, per se.  It's an issue w/ serialization and shared memory in python multiprocesing pools.  Consider this example:

```python
from multiprocessing import Pool
import os

class FooObject(object): pass

def foo(*args):
  fooObj = FooObject()
  print "PID {} created: {}".format(os.getpid(), id(fooObj))
  return fooObj

def main():
  pool = Pool(3)
  results = pool.map(foo, range(3))
  for result in results:
    print "PID {} Result: {}".format(os.getpid(), id(result))

if __name__ == "__main__":
  main()
```

The output for which is:

```
PID 1368 created: 4450700048
PID 1369 created: 4450700112
PID 1368 created: 4450699984
PID 1367 Result: 4450800016
PID 1367 Result: 4450800272
PID 1367 Result: 4450800336
```

The _objects_ returned by `Pool.map()` are not the same, actual instances created in the `foo()` function.  I don't know what guarantees, if any, we offer about the state of objects between processes, which would require full serialize/deserialize support (which is an on-going, and in-progress issue).

Applying it to this specific problem, the objects created in `createModel()` are not the same objects in the main process.  CLA Model state is persisted and shared between processes by saving in the process in which it was created, and loaded in another process in which it is used, similar to the way `htmengine` handles checkpoints during model swapping.

@hohoCode I think that if those two (or more) fields are preprocessed in the same way, then you don't need two (or more) different fields, but only a single one. For example, if there are two text columns in your `TabularDataset` and, let's say, a label column, you could use a single text field **twice** and a single label field once in the specification. That is:

```
TEXT  = data.Field(tokenize=data.get_tokenizer("moses"), lower=True)
LABEL  = data.Field(sequential=False, use_vocab=False)

FIELDS = [
    ("sentence1", TEXT),
    ("sentence2", TEXT),
    ("label", LABEL)
]

dataset = data.TabularDataset(path="something.csv", format="tsv", fields=FIELDS)
```

Now, you need to build the vocabulary only once. Let me know if this helped (and worked).

Cryptography builds an empty wheel on pypy < 2.6 due to a too-old version of cffi; this wheel then gets cached on Travis making all other builds break. I think you need named assertions if you are using Z3 in incremental mode. You can get z3 to produce an output by doing the following modifications:

First, add the following on top:

```
(set-option :produce-interpolants true)
```

Then, instead of using `compute-interpolants`, assert the facts with names and use `get-interpolant`, like this:

```
(assert (! (and (= x@0 0) (= y@0 1) (= n 0)) :named formula1))
(assert (! (and mainNegMacro0 (and (not %lbl%@227) %lbl%+166 %lbl%+88 %lbl%+90 %lbl%+212 %lbl%+94) (= AbsHoudiniConstant1 true) (= AbsHoudiniConstant0 true)) :named formula2))
(check-sat)
(get-interpolant formula1 formula2)
```

After these mods, Z3 responds:

```
(interpolants
 true)
```

Now, I can't opine on whether this output is correct or not; or whether it is what you intended. But this should get you going I think. See https://rise4fun.com/iZ3/tutorialcontent/guide#h24 for details on this.

```
selected_spatial_action_flat = np.argmax(flattened_spatial_output_from_convnet)
```

```
actions.FunctionCall(move_action_id, [[0], selected_spatial_action_flat_2d[::-1]])
```

```
actions.FunctionCall(move_action_id, [[0], selected_spatial_action_flat_2d])
``` 

@pzanitti Enforcing unique=True makes assumptions about multiple users sharing the same device. Out of the box the DPNS DRF API grants the registration_id to the first user who registers it. The DPNS DRF API will raise an exception, _APNS device with Registration ID already exists_, if another user tries to register their account (with push service) on the same device.

I've had to debug this scenario frequently enough with customers that I disabled the unique=True on APNS and didn't look back. Plus, DPNS does not enforce it on GCM/FCM so having it on one and not the other is confusing to developers.

I have 2 recommendations for solving this problem:
1. The simplest solution would be to edit the resource layer and change the query method (in list) and before_get_object method (in detail). Your query should relate the current user and join on the table fetching the resources according to user.
2. Define row level security at database level. This is even simpler (but difficult to setup at first) and more secure way of solving this problem. I use this in most of my use cases but I am not aware about the feature compatibility with other databases. Postgres supports it. You can follow the code on this [link](https://github.com/subzerocloud/postgrest-starter-kit/tree/master/db) to know how things work. It can be quite difficult to understand at first. So in brief this is what it does:
a. There is a `data` schema which stores all your main tables.
b. Subset of these tables are viewed in `api` layer which is being accessed by your code. Thus data layer is completely protected.
c. `api` role is given access to edit the `data` tables (but not all you can control it).
d. Roles are stored as database types and are created on database initialisations. They are granted to `authenticator` role which has access to only switch roles I suppose (not clear about this).
e. Other roles are given access to edit the `api` schema views and also row level security is enabled which doing that.

So basically you can control which columns can a user see and which rows a user can see or edit with this. It will be difficult to set up but secure and fun to learn.

Let me start by clarifying that I don't use CAS so I'm referring to SSO principles in general.

The application probably works one of two ways (determined by the plugin that manages Django auth):

1. It authenticates once (initially) - you will remain logged into the application so long as you don't timeout within the application
1. It reauthenticates periodically - if the SSO session ends, you will be prompted to re-authenticate at some point

However, you would expect the timer on the SSO session to be reset each time the application reauthenticates.  It follows that you shouldn't be abruptly terminated by either approach (unless the SSO timeout is shorter than the application reauth time).

Hmm, the likely answer is that those contracts were created by other contracts (factory contracts) and not external transactions. If that's true then I don't see any easy way to add those addresses.

One idea though, this here is from [StackOverflow](https://ethereum.stackexchange.com/questions/760/how-is-the-address-of-an-ethereum-contract-computed):

```
The address for an Ethereum contract is deterministically computed from the address of its creator (sender) and how many transactions the creator has sent (nonce). The sender and nonce are RLP encoded and then hashed with Keccak-256.
```

What we theoretically could do is

1. for every contract address, check if another contract created by that contract exists. Would be expensive though because lots more SHA3 hashes to compute, also we'd also have to test multiple nonces.

2. concretely execute every transaction that goes to an existing contract and check whether it creates another contract, and if it does add that address to the index.

Both of those are difficult to do, so at the moment it's probably not worth the effort?

Benchmarking script (based off @mark-adams 's found in https://github.com/atlassian/asap-authentication-python/pull/92) 
```
import timeit

import atlassian_jwt_auth
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa, ec
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.serialization import Encoding
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.serialization import PrivateFormat
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization


def wrapper(func, *args, **kwargs):
    def wrapped():
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
    return wrapped


def sign(signer):
    signer.generate_jwt('audience')


def main():
    ec_private_key = ec.generate_private_key(
        ec.SECP256R1(), default_backend())

    rsa_private_key = rsa.generate_private_key(
        public_exponent=65537,
        key_size=2048,
        backend=default_backend()
    )

    ec_private_private_pem = ec_private_key.private_bytes(
        encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        format=serialization.PrivateFormat.TraditionalOpenSSL,
        encryption_algorithm=serialization.NoEncryption()
    )

    rsa_private_key_pem = rsa_private_key.private_bytes(
        Encoding.PEM, PrivateFormat.PKCS8, serialization.NoEncryption()
    )
    issuer = 'issuer'
    key_id = 'issuer/key'

    ec_signer = atlassian_jwt_auth.create_signer(
        issuer, key_id, ec_private_private_pem, algorithm='ES256'
    )
    rsa_signer = atlassian_jwt_auth.create_signer(
        issuer, key_id, rsa_private_key_pem, algorithm='RS256'
    )
    ec_w = wrapper(sign, ec_signer)
    rsa_w = wrapper(sign, rsa_signer)
    num = 10000
    print('ec', timeit.timeit(ec_w, number=num))
    print('rsa', timeit.timeit(rsa_w, number=num))


main()
```

@tthomas0702 this gets the point across.

Here's my point. You mention that you are able to post the entire template using postman. The fact of the matter is that you posted a single template, and that template is special because nowhere in it are fields such as "presentation", "macro", "html-help", "role-acl", etc.

Your template there is only an `implementation`.

An iApp template has a loose specification, but a basic set of fields that it can take. For example, see this stub.

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-ansible/blob/devel/test/integration/targets/bigip_iapp_template/files/basic-iapp.tmpl

You have provided only a single one of those fields, `implementation`. Were your iApp to have any number of other fields, you would not be able to give the iApp to the REST api in the manner that you did. A `presentation` field cannot go in an `implementation` field.

This is the crux of the problem with the REST API for iApp templates and the reason that I suggest you avoid using it entirely. The iApps that F5 releases contain all those fields in that example and more. Some of the additional fields are not even settable via the iApp template API and need to be set at other APIS (such as procs).

If you have one of those iapps, then the burden of parsing this file (such as this https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-ansible/blob/devel/test/integration/targets/bigip_iapp_template/files/f5.microsoft_exchange_2016.v1.0.0.tmpl) is entirely on your shoulders. Because the valid REST representation of that iApp is

```
tm.sys.application.templates_template.create(
    implementation=YOUR_PARSED_IMPLEMENTATION_BLOCK,
    presentation=YOUR_PARSED_PRESENTATION_BLOCK,
    htmlHelp=YOUR_PARSED_HTML_HELP_BLOCK,
    macros=YOUR_PARSED_MACROS_BLOCK
)
as well as 10's or more API calls to create each proc at the expected APIs.
```

This is __hard__ and no customer would ever expect to have to do this...I mean...F5 gave me this iApp, why do I need to parse it? That's crazy talk to expect that. And yet, this is the API.

So to mitigate that problem, the approach I recommend is to not use the API at all at this time. Instead, use the method of put the iApp on the box via an upload, and then use tmsh over REST to install it. At least until such time as a new API exists that behaves in a similar manner (or they fix the existing one because it is, arguably, unusable).

@craigmillernz, thank you for the investigative work finding that v1.1.12 causes this issue. I used this to track down this issue's origins. 

### The Culprit:

When I merged the `SurferGridReader`, I added an unnecessary SciPy import (😳) which I will remove and re-deploy by tomorrow.

SciPy requires a version of NumPy greater than the version that gets shipped with ParaView. Since ParaView's application contents get searched for site-packages first, ParaView tries to use the older version of NumPy.

### The Solution:

In the next patch release (v1.1.16), the crashing issue will be resolved, the package will have `try`/`catch`es for the methods that require SciPy and NumPy>=1.10.x, and I will implement a test suite on Travis CI to test on NumPy<=1.10.x to prevent a release that will cause this kind of crash.

To fully use the rest of PVGeo's features and to have slightly faster IO with the `ubc` suite, you will still need to delete ParaView's local version of NumPy. Some of PVGeo's algorithms require a version of NumPy above 1.10 which will be installed in the `PVGeoEnv` virtual environment alongside PVGeo. However, the `ubc` suite will perform fine be without this.

#### Deleting ParaView's NumPy:

On Windows: navigate to ParaView's application contents then go to `bin\Lib\site-packages\` and delete the `numpy` directory.

@craigmillernz, from the photo attached above, yours would be under:

`C:\Program Files\ParaView 5.5.2-666-g1283e995e8-Qt5-Windows-msvc2015-64bit\bin\Lib\site-packages\`

### Further Action:

This is obviously not a preferred solution... users shouldn't need to dive into ParaView's application contents and delete something. To mitigate this, I will raise an issue with ParaView to see if I can stir some action to upgrade the version of NumPy that gets shipped with ParaView on Windows.

> Should the debit occur when the preorder is committed? Otherwise, an adversary could preorder a bunch of names, and only issue a register when they see another user's register hit the mempool without penalty (in STACKs). Granted, the limited validity window for preorders limits the scope of this, but I wonder if this is something we want to deal with.

Trouble is, it's impossible to know anything about the name while it is preordered (including determining which namespace it was in), except perhaps the price.  I agree that ideally, a preorder would burn all the STACKs up front.  This could be done if we change the wire format of the preorder to include the number of STACKs to burn, for example.  However, I'm also trying to avoid that so we don't end up having to patch blockstack.js significantly (i.e. the wire formats of all these transactions are still the same in this description).  What do you think?

the other option would be to redefine the "strict" pattern:

* `[0-9a-z_]+` => `[0-9A-Za-z_]+`

I think this makes sense anyways, as we do allow a leading digit, which is _not_ a valid identifier in most programming languages.

so above would allow for:

* identifiers (as in most programming languages), in any case mixing
* complete numerical (digits) strings
* hex strings (lower and upper cased)

but it still doesn't allow for eg base64:

* `[^-A-Za-z0-9+/=]|=[^=]|={3,}$` or
* `^(?:[A-Za-z0-9+/]{4})*(?:[A-Za-z0-9+/]{2}==|[A-Za-z0-9+/]{3}=)?$`

source: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/475074/regex-to-parse-or-validate-base64-data

---

actually, I think a better user experience could be:

* by _default_, use the "loose" patterns, which only exclude whitespace, `#` and `.` (URI parts can be anything matching `[^\s\.#]*`)
* let the use switch to "strict", and have that use a pattern that results in valid identifiers for most programming languages

I don't know about the cross-platform compatibility, but the autobahn.asyncio.websocket.WebSocketServerFactory (which is a dedicated class for use with asyncio) is explicitly accepting a loop keyword in its constructor and the usage of the `Future()` without a loop keyword breaks the promise of that factory's constructor.

As for an example usage with multiple event loops: I am currently working on a deployment script that can manage multiple services, each running in a separate thread, and each service (possibly) using different main-loop implementations. So this library needs to be able to handle a deployment of (I'm making up an example scenario here) four different services, where one service implements a threaded web-server where the main thread just accepts connections and spawns a new thread for each connection, an FTP service that makes use of an ftp library that operates using calls to select.epoll(), and two other services that use asyncio, but possibly using different helper libraries.

The library in this scenario has no clue whatsoever what technologies are being used for each service and the services don't know about each other's implementations, either. This is why each of these service implementations must be able to manage a dedicated event loop in my case.

I encountered this bug during the implementation of an autobahn-powered websocket service.
Autobahn includes a WebSocket library, but requires either Twisted or asyncio. We won't port Autobahn to anything else.

That situation is already present in 6.1.16, it locks the object tree so that only one thread can interact with its schema items at once. This PR doesn't make any functional changes to the external expected behaviour, it is intended to simplify the acquisition of object locks to reduce the chances of deadlocks.

Presently, acquiring a child lock in one thread while another thread is printing the root object will cause a deadlock (I think I've seen this during submit for example). This PR solves this problem by only having a single lock per object tree.

LGTM 👍

Overall looks good.

This change can be helpful for simple Thread routers or end-devices. 

If the use-case requires a client/driver interfacing to OpenThread, then I think it'd be better to have it be in charge of restarting OpenThread/NCP. If OpenThread/NCP starts on its own before the client/driver is ready, driver may miss some of the events/callbacks from OT (e.g., new on-mesh prefix, new address, etc.) or some of the received data packets.

closing this one in favor of #3565

I don't think it'd make sense to convert panics into exceptions, as panics by nature are showstoppers - so just crashing seems reasonable/expected. I also don't believe all panics can be caught reliably - just those affected by `std::panic::catch_unwind`. beyond that, simply ensuring we don't use panic-able calls like `unwrap` or `expect` where we're remotely likely to hit them would be good practice.

The first use of this should probably be to implement https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/5634 - this is just being able to introspect snapshots.

The second should probably be to remote the explicit `PathGlobs` calls from https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/tests/python/pants_test/engine/test_isolated_process.py and instead specify the input snapshots using this new API. This covers basic creation of snapshots from files.

The third should be to specify the list of input files for executing `cloc` remotely. This covers unioning of snapshots (combining a path glob with the cloc script itself). It also covers introspecting snapshots (as the output of cloc is produced as a file in the output snapshot).

After those three things are done, this API is probably sufficiently complete for us to close out this ticket.

Another reason this PR was left pending is that it's a kind of breaking change. And if we're going to break things, then it would make the most sense to try to identify as many legacy pain points as possible and target a new (major?) release that would bundle all the backward-incompatible changes together. Then, the API could again remain constant for the foreseeable future.

Also, something to figure out is what to do with the arguments (such as `context`) that already exist throughout the call chain. In #316, I floated the idea that `context` should go inside `env` too, which got tentative support from @kstrauser.

The way I envisioned this, individual arguments (existing and future) should remain available in methods that are typically directly called by application code using Schematics. For example, `Model.__init__` would still take `strict` and `partial` arguments. But in the majority of the other ("internal") methods, particularly those inside type classes, these would be absent and `env` would propagate the values.

OAuth tokens are linked to the application client-id. So even if the client-id in not used globally and users are required to authenticate, Twitch still knows which application they are using. Revoking a client-id will also invalidate all oauth tokens associated with it.
There is no choice here. You can either make it simple for everyone or make it difficult by requiring annoying setup steps.
That's why I think it's important to add a config option for the client-id (`--twitch-client-id`), so that it can easily be switched if the user decides to or in case it was revoked, without the need of using the `--http-header` option.

I don't know how I feel about alerting the users that are using the default client-id. It's not a terrible idea, as it would reduce the number of people using the default one, but I'm not sure if people will actually care. They would need to register their own application on Twitch or use an oauth token registered by a different app with the sufficient API access permissions.

> Please do not request comprehensive sets of icons related to a very specific field.

I have to agree with the rest of the crowd that CC icons aren't a narrow focus.

To me, an example of narrow focus are game controller button icons. A series of icons representing the buttons of all gamepad controllers would be very specific. Only video game sites that illustrating specific commands would need the approved icon for Left Bumper or "Option" button. Very narrow in focus and application.

Creative Commons on the other hand applies to a large amount of media in a large amount of contexts. Just as ™ or ® can be applied to any work, Creative Commons licenses have the same scope.

I think the right fix is to propagate the queue length for resources to the autoscaler, that way we can make a decision correctly when the current capacity is 0.

Btw, there are a couple workarounds for now:
1. Use `ray create_or_update cluster.yaml --min-workers=1 --no-restart` to force a resize when needed.
2. Launch a bunch of CPU tasks at the start of your job to force scale-up, e.g.

```
@ray.remote(num_cpus=1)
def f():
   time.sleep(10)

[f.remote() for _ in range(100)]
```

@yelosheng 
我来献丑详细的解释一下:)

* 以 google.com 为例

常规的 http 或者是 https 请求被墙(假设它没有被墙)的网站
----------------------------------------

1. 首先你在浏览器里面键入 google.com, 然后回车
2. 浏览器发起 DNS 请求获取 google.com 的 **IP 地址**(因为TCP连接必须要ip地址, 域名只是为了让人好记忆而发明出来的), DNS 服务器查询 google.com 的 IP 地址, 然后返回给浏览器.
3. 浏览器拿到了 google.com 的IP地址, 然后向这个 IP 地址发起 TCP 连接, 三次握手之后连接成功.
4. 然后到了 HTTP 协议的时间了, 浏览器向 google 的服务器发送 HTTP 请求头(因为此时 TCP 连接已经建立, 可以发送数据了), 请求头其实就是一段字符串.

```plaintext
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: www.google.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows; U; Windows NT 5.1; en-US; rv:1.7.6)
Gecko/20050225 Firefox/1.0.1
Connection: Keep-Alive
...
```

无论是 HTTP 请求还是加密的 HTTPS 请求, **请求头都是不会被加密的**.

5. google.com 收到请求头, 如果是 HTTPS 请求, 还要交换秘钥什么的, 这个就不解释了, 总之就是 google.com 把数据返回回来了.

我们看一下上述流程 墙 可以做什么
----------------------------------

1. 我们键入 google.com 的时候浏览器需要先发起 DNS 请求查询 google.com 的 IP 地址, 如果拿不到这个 IP 地址, 不就无法和 google.com 的真实 IP 地址通讯了吗, 所以 墙 的初期手段就是污染 DNS, AI, 浏览器任你发送 DNS 请求, 国内的 DNS 服务器就给你返回一个错误的 IP 地址, 什么冰岛的,什么澳大利亚的地址给你, 去访问吧, 浏览器很懵, 有可能这个 IP 地址压根就没有被绑定服务器, 然后服务器不可达. 但是 DNS 请求需要我们的浏览器发起, 既然 DNS 请求能被污染, 如果我自己知道正确的 google.com IP地址, 我来告诉浏览器正确的 IP 地址不就行了吗, 刚开始想要科学上网的人确实是这么做的, 在 window 系统 `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc` 该目录下面有一个 `hosts` 文件, Mac 系统下面的路径为 `/etc/hosts`, 如果操作系统能在这里获得关于域名的 IP 地址, 就不会向 DNS 服务器发起请求了, 所以都赶忙去找 google.com 的真实 IP 地址填在这里来让操作系统不要去其他的 DNS 查询了. 这个时期应该是成功的度过了一段时间吧, 当然我没有经历过, 我想科学上网的时候 google.com 的 IP 地址已经被封了🧐.
忘了一件事情, 因为 DNS 服务器在公网上面有很多, 而且都是免费的, 出名的 `8.8.8.8` 就是谷歌提供的, 你可以自己填写自己的操作系统的 DNS 地址, 应该有一段时期可以把 DNS 服务器修改为 `8.8.8.8`, 谷歌并不会返回错误的地址, 所以后来 `8.8.8.8` 也被墙了👽.

2. 墙 继续升级, 既然你们能搞到真实的 google.com 的 IP 地址, 那我就把所有已知的 google.com 的 IP 地址全部封了, 但是 google.com 的服务器是可以换 IP 地址的, 你可以试一下, 多访问几次 google.com, 有可能是不相同的, 所以只是把已知的 google.com 服务器的 IP 地址封掉并不能完全的阻挡, 所以 墙 从 HTTP 协议入手了, HTTP 协议请求头的发送全部是明文的, 无论是 HTTP, 还是 HTTPS, 请求头全部都是明文的, 请求头里面有 `google.com`, 这就很尴尬了, 这个域名没有办法隐藏, 墙 很开心, 好了, 所有经过我这里的数据包都要被拆开看看你访问的地址是不是**违禁**地址, 如果是的就不要继续了, 墙 会模拟 google.com 的服务器给你的电脑发送一个 `RST` TCP响应, 电脑的TCP协议看到这个响应就会无条件的重置连接, 所以你会看到有时候你能拿到正确的 google.com 的IP地址, 也会看到浏览器提示你**连接被重置**, 这时候的 RST 的响应可是墙发给你的, 是不是感觉很荣耀😀, 然后 墙 还会模仿客户端给 google.com 的服务器发送 RST 响应, google.com 以为是你发的, 也会无条件的关闭 TCP 连接.

看一下 shadowsocks 做了什么
------------------------------

有一个代理协议叫做 `socks5`, 可以帮助你穿透防火墙, 由 socks5 负责传递双方的数据

```shell
client ---------> socks5 -----------> server
```

shadowsocks 把 socks5 拆成了两个部分

```shell
client -------> ssclient ------> ssserver ---------> server
```

client 把数据传递给 `ssclient`, 这就是你为什么要把 socks5 客户端的地址要填写 `ssclient` 监听的地址, `ssclient` 把数据加密, 包括 DNS 请求什么的一切数据全部加密发送给 `ssserver`(并且 ss 没有握手阶段, 没有明显的数据特征能辨识这个是 ss 流量), `ssserver` 把数据解密拿到 `client` 想要访问的域名, 然后发起 DNS 请求获取 IP 地址(这也就是为什么你的 VPS 要放在国外的原因), 然后和这个 IP 地址握手, 然后服务器响应, `ssserver` 并不负责处理数据, 只是原样把数据加密回传给 `ssclient`, `ssclient` 解密数据然后传递给浏览器, 浏览器负责数据的辨识处理(因为 HTTPS 还需要进一步的握手).

结尾

当然在Linux Desktop上安装Chrome后，SSServer从SSLocal收到的是未解析的网址，这样SSServer主机不启动IPv6也没有问题。
这就不是Shadowsocks开发上的问题，而是为什么Firefox会将网址解析成IPv6再发给代理。

cobc will try to invoke the same compiler as the one that was used to build it. If you're using a MinGW build of OpenCobol, cobc will use gcc to compile the generated c file. If you're using a Visual Studio build of OpenCobol, it will use the visual studio c compiler to compile the generated c file.

OpenCobolIDE ships with a mingw build of OpenCobol: http://www.opencobol.org/modules/bwiki/index.php?Assorted%20Documents (mingw is bundled too). The IDE just setup it's own environment variable so that the compiler can be detected (and other mingw installation ignored) and can work out of the box without requiring the user to setup anything.

> P.S. while on linkedin someone was saying there were some problems with environment variables and large programs using IDE Is this the case, if so has it been fixed, so far can give you no further information and don't know at this point if it a known issue. Let me know and I can check it out.

There was an environment variable issue with an old version( almost one year ago) that prevented the IDE from using the bundled mingw installation (the user's mingw version was used instead of the bundled one, leading to strange errors). I don't know anything about problems with large projects. I can only suppose they say that because the IDE works on a single file basis and does not have any concept of project (yet). See https://github.com/OpenCobolIDE/OpenCobolIDE/issues/84

Thanks for this PR. I've had a good long look at how the template resolution currently works and think we need to do a bit more work here, basically along the lines of what is proposed in #1378. My view is that:

1. Oscar internally has to be consistent about how it loads templates. Currently with this PR some templates are extended with an `oscar/` prefix, but others are not. This is problematic because different template loaders will be used in template resolution (anything with the `oscar/` prefix will go through the Filesystem loader; anything without will go through the AppDirectories loader). This inconsistency will cause all sorts of confusion with projects that have apps that collide with any Oscar templates.

2. Oscar should *not* hog the global template namespace, and should be prefixing everything with `oscar/`.

On this basis I think the best approach is to do pretty much was #1378 says:

1. Change *all* Oscar templates to extend `oscar/` prefixed templates.

2. Change `OSCAR_MAIN_TEMPLATE_DIR` to point to `src/oscar` instead of `src/oscar/templates/oscar`. 

This is a backwards-incompatible change, but we probably just have to make it at some point.

On 5 Mar. 2018 07:49, "Rob Woodcock" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> The ODC can just Index or it can Ingest (which is index+slice). The
> principle reason for ingestion is the data can be transformed from what is
> usually an archival data storage format into a computationally efficient
> form. With repeated use and tuned data storage forms the ODC can achieve
> higher overall performance compared with Index only.
>
> How much of an improvement varies depending on the primary analytics
> use-cases, archive storage form and deployed environment. For example, the
> CSIRO deployment of the ODC on AWS ingests the archival form of the data
> (in most cases scenes or swaths in geotiff or netcdf) into AWS S3 object
> storage using the ODC S3 Array IO storage plugin. S3 is cheaper than file
> storage and with the S3AIO plugin scales better with increased
> computational nodes. The CSIRO deployment tends to treat the ingest form of
> that data as a caching mechanism where our most frequently used data is
> available. In future we would like to make the cache management automatic
> and on demand.
>
> Alternately, the Digital Earth Australia deployment on the NCI High
> Performance Computing system has a shared archive of EO data. The data
> needs to be compatible with a range of non-ODC applications. The ODC uses
> this data in place as netcdf files via indexing only. It is efficient as
> the NCI HPC system has tuned these files to be performant in that
> environment whilst being compatible with a range of applications, of which
> ODC is one. There is no need to ingest in this deployment.
>
> So sometimes, just indexing is easy, if you have a need for an archival
> form. The ODC will work with both index only and ingested forms. Which is
> more efficient depends on your environment and use-cases.
>

I agree on the consistency aspect, and would definitely expect an `AttributeError` on attribute access, I don't have a good answer there yet, maybe an `AttrDictAttributeError` which will keep the original exception.. I'm not sure if it's possible.

The problem is due to the nature of attrdicts having the tendancy to consider attribute failure as a _missing attribute_ rather than an actual exception. This makes the first access of an attribute on an attrdict the initial error. 

The places where this will bite you in the ass on a complicated model are plenty, a simple case:

```python
from elasticsearch_dsl import DocType
class Product(DocType):
    pass

class Category(DocType):
    @property
    def products(self):
        return Product.all()

category = Category(name='laptop')
category.products
```
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/dan/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl/document.py", line 166, in __getattr__
    return super(DocType, self).__getattr__(name)
  File "/home/dan/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl/utils.py", line 374, in __getattr__
    return super(ObjectBase, self).__getattr__(name)
  File "/home/dan/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch_dsl/utils.py", line 81, in __getattr__
    '%r object has no attribute %r' % (self.__class__.__name__, attr_name))
AttributeError: 'Category' object has no attribute 'products'
```

Category *does* have an attribute products, `Product.all` is missing - but the exception will make no mention of this. Consider a complex model with methods/attributes all depending on each other. The result is wasting time following the chain of events, which is what we have tracebacks for in the first place.

The problem is that IDs get are generated in a way that gives elements with the most references the shortest IDs. When running the second time on an already processed file the shortest IDs are therefore already "taken" and replaced with potentially longer ones.

What we need to do is assign "temporary" IDs to all elements and/or exchange IDs when necessary, but it needs some investigation on how to do this efficiently.

Noticeable changes:
* Created `SafeContext` class - to separate the chain and overwriting values logic. `Chain` can use other context types (dict)
* merge `Chain.__call__` and `Chain.resolve`

Our motivation has to do with a strong policy around protecting user data. We want our logging aggregation systems (we're using loggly) to be accessible to developers and front-line support but we also want to be able to say that we don't share user data with anyone, including internal employees.
One of the ways that user data leaks out of a system is if a developer includes it in a log. For example, if we decided that a user's name was sensitive, we'd want to ensure that our logs contained an opaque `user_id`, never the user name.
The implementation approaches this in two ways:
 1. By trying to prevent the use of string concatenation in logs (vs explicit variable passing in the standard logging `extra` dictionary)
 2. By providing an (optional) mechanism for whitelisting which field names may appear in the `extra` dictionary

Note that this *doesn't* prevent developers from doing something like:

```
extra=dict(
    user_id=user.name,
)
```

But at least once you get to that point you have a straightforward story around code review, PRs, documented policies, and common sense that will hold up to scrutiny.

Due to new developments, this code must be revisited, and more research done to match w3c webdriver standards. I have plans to do that after Python 3 compatibility is mainstream. I do not see the need to have it as a plugin, because it is accepted to be part of S2L.

The Open Browser is not intended to handle all the possibilities which can be used to create the driver instance from selenium. It is intended to be a simple and easy way to create the default driver.

Creating the driver is actually quite complex scenario and there is lots of different options for different browsers. Although the Create Webdriver is powerful, it cannot handle all the options, fine example is in #532.

If one has special needs, which is not supported by the Open Browser keyword, then the next option is to go with the Create Webdriver keyword.  If it comes to complex to handle in RF data or need cannot be fulfilled by the Create Webdriver keyword, one can always extend the S2L and create a new keyword to open the browser.

```python
def parse_env(envlist):
    '''parse_env will parse a single line (with prefix like ENV removed) to
    a list of commands in the format KEY=VALUE For example:

        ENV PYTHONBUFFER 1 --> [PYTHONBUFFER=1]

    ::Notes
      Docker: https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/builder/#env

    '''
    if not isinstance(envlist, list):
        envlist = [envlist]

    exports = [] 

    for env in envlist:
        pieces = re.split("( |\\\".*?\\\"|'.*?')", env)
        pieces = [p for p in pieces if p.strip()]

        while len(pieces) > 0:
            current = pieces.pop(0)
            if current.endswith('='):
                next = pieces.pop(0)
                exports.append("%s%s" %(current, next))
            elif '=' in current:
                exports.append(current)
            else:
                next = pieces.pop(0)
                exports.append("%s=%s" %(current, next))

    return exports
```

This package no longer has a dependency on `persistent`, so the original patch, which added `PersistentList` and `PersistentDict` to the `_type` attributes of `List` and `Dict` won't work (which are currently just `list` and `dict`, respectively).

What if those `_type` attributes were specified as `collections.MutableSequence` and `collections.MutableMapping`? `Persistent[List|Dict]` are instances of those classes, and users can register or create more.

The `IList` interface doesn't say that only a real, honest-to-goodness `list` object is acceptable, it says " a value that implements the API of a conventional Python list." `IDict` is a bit more restrictive, saying it holds "a conventional dict," but we could fudge that 😄

```Python
class PIDStatus(Enum):
    """Constants for possible status of any given PID."""

    __order__ = 'NEW RESERVED REGISTERED REDIRECTED DELETED'

    NEW = ('N', _('New'))
    """PID has *not* yet been registered with the service provider."""

    RESERVED = ('K', _('Reserved'))
    """PID reserved in the service provider but not yet fully registered."""

    REGISTERED = ('R', _('Registered'))
    """PID has been registered with the service provider."""

    REDIRECTED = ('M', _('Redirected'))
    """PID has been redirected to another persistent identifier."""

    DELETED = ('D', _('Deleted'))
    """PID has been deleted/inactivated with the service provider.

    This should happen very rarely, and must be kept track of, as the PID
    should not be reused for something else.
    """

    def __init__(self, char, title):
        """Hack."""
        self.char = char
        self.title = title

    def __eq__(self, other):
        """Equality test."""
        return self.char == other

    def __str__(self):
        """Return its character value."""
        return self.char
```

I've been struggling with this the last couple days and I've noticed some things.

Indeed there is a problem with "t" character since it's listed as a keyword. The strange thing is that 
`find (title top  or top-quark or top-antitop or t) and (a abachi or abbott or abazov) and cn d0` 
seems to work. Actually the query parses fine if "t" is either the first or the last argument. Parsing right when its the first argument is logical since after a keyword (title i.e.) a value is expected (so it treats "t" as a value). 

https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-query-parser/blob/master/invenio_query_parser/contrib/spires/parser.py#L231-L238


The problem is that when the "t" is at the middle of the query the parser expects to identify something like:

`find t Something or t SomethingElse`

so when after a "t" a "or" appears it "thinks" that it's a mistake. 

I hope I helped somehow. I will continue searching for the problem in case I figure it out.

Currently since reconstructions are rather difficult to guess a priori
without experimental input, the ReconstructionGenerator on surface.py just
takes in a conventional unit cell and determines the spacegroup of the cell
(and wykoff positions if we want to deal with more complicated
reconstructions, I'll have this implement soon) and applies a set of site
translations, deletions, additions and supercell transformations to a slab
from instructions provided in a json file. The instructions are based on
already well-known reconstructions. The generate_all_slabs function has an
option to include the reconstructions which is what the workflow in my
previous fork of atomate uses. Currently, I'm trying to incorporate a
similar framework for reconstructions into the workflow me and Joey have
been working on. The incorporation of LOCPOT was so the workfunction can be
obtained by default for all slab calculations since this is a useful
surface properties for both clean and adsorbed surfaces. This is just a
minor edit to MVLSlabSet, the workflow will handle the rest.

Thanks,
Richard

Remember to document all modules, classes, and functions with docstrings! See [this pr](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/12/files#diff-454f1a8594a4a7bb39db9eb666828811R1) by way of example. At a minimum, we should add docstrings to those functions described in the spec. For the format, I like the rst-style docstrings (which are automatically created by PyCharm) which list parameters and return types like this:

```python
def foo(bar, baz):
    """
    Describe foo in one or two sentences here. Does it raise any exceptions?

    :param bar: Describe expected values of bar
    :param baz: And baz
    :return: Describe what is returned -- a tuple? A string? `None`? Something else?
    """
```

Before 'delete' was a feature, re-importing was the only way to update a channel to the latest state from the CC server. On import:
* the latest version of the DB is downloaded, overwriting the old one. (that's why I expected the 'updated' time to change)
* subsequently, any newly-referenced content is also downloaded

I definitely agree that we need clearer feedback to the user about when updates are available, and whether anything actually changed on re-import.

I know this Issue is old and closed, but in case anybody else comes looking for this and finds this issue, here is a function to generate a new schema instance with its `partial` set to True, along with any nested schemas, recursively. You could easily update it to make it a class factory, as well.

```python
def fully_partial(schema, _exclude=()):
    """Return a fully partial schema instance.

    A fully partial schema instance is a schema instance that is
    set to ``partial=True``, along with any schemas specified as
    nested fields, recursively.

    This should work both on ``Nested`` fields and custom fields
    inheriting from ``Nested``.

    :param schema: a schema instance or schema class to convert to
        a fully partial schema.
    :param _exclude: private parameter used in recursion. Do not
        supply unless you're sure of what you're doing.
    """
    sch_cls = schema.__class__
    new_sch = type(
        'FullyPartial{}'.format(schema.__class__.__name__),
        (sch_cls,),
        dict(_nested_partial_fields(schema, exclude=_exclude))
    )
    return new_sch(partial=True)


def _nested_partial_fields(schema, exclude):
    """Return fields for fully_partial.

    Returns all non-excluded field names and fields unchanged from 
    the provided schema, except for nested fields, for which the nested
    schema is replaced with one where ``partial=True``.

    :param schema: an instantiated schema
    :param exclude: fields to exclude from the new schema
    """
    for field_name, field in schema.fields.items():
        f_attrs = {
            f: field.__dict__[f] for
            f in field.__dict__ if not
            f.startswith('_')
        }
        if field_name in exclude:
            continue

        if isinstance(field, Nested):
            new_sch = fully_partial(field.schema, _exclude=field.exclude)
            f_attrs['nested'] = new_sch
            new_field = Nested(**f_attrs)
        else:
            new_field = field.__class__(**f_attrs)
        yield field_name, new_field
```

One use case that would be missed if we deprecate `strict=False` would be accessing partially valid data. You can currently do the following:

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class MySchema(Schema):
    always_valid = fields.Field()
    always_invalid = fields.Field(validate=[lambda val: False])


sch = MySchema()

data = {'always_valid': 42, 'always_invalid': 24}

res = sch.load(data)

# Partially valid data
print(res.data)  # => {'always_valid': 42}
# Error messages
print(res.errors)  # => {'always_invalid': ['Invalid value.']}
```

A simple solution would be to store partially valid data on `ValidationError`, so that you could do:

```python

try:
    data = schema.load(data)
except ValidationError as err:
    # Partially valid data
    valid_data = err.valid_data
    # Error messages
    errors = err.messages
    # Raw input data
    raw_data = err.data
```

@ryanlowe0 I like the idea of making `raw_data` optional (option 1) for backwards compat, at least for the time being; we may decide to make the argument required in a future release.

```python
#...
if isinstance(func, functools.partial):
    argspec = inspect.getargspec(func.func)
return argspec.args
```

It should also handle callable objects (i.e. inspect `obj.__call__` when appropriate).

Hi @rmandelb .  Sorry so slow to get to this.  I'll try to go through it this weekend so we can potentially discuss next week.

A couple quick comments:

> @jmeyers314 , one thing I was thinking about was the question of whether it makes sense to ever draw a ChromaticAtmosphere or ChromaticOpticalPSF or any such thing without convolving with something that has an SED.

From a physics point-of-view, I think you're right.  From a code point-of-view I'm not sure if we want to explicitly check this.  I guess the original design in my head was for evaluateAtWavelength() to encapsulate all of the wavelength-dependence.  The fact that separable objects (including `GSObject`s) have an `.SED` attribute is more of an exception than a rule.  The SED attribute is there only because its presence allows the separable profile to be drawn more quickly.  Also, none of the existing code attempts to access `.SED` unless the object is also marked as `separable`.

As an example, the `ChromaticSum` class doesn't have an SED attribute, and I'm pretty sure the `setupInterpolation` method will fail for `ChromaticSum` right now since the former assumes that `.SED` exists.

>since evaluateAtWavelength for the ChromaticOpticalPSF class does not explicitly apply transformations, nothing would happen here

I'm not sure I understand.  If `ChromaticOpticalPSF.dilate()` were left undefined (to take one example), then the parent class method `ChromaticObject.dilate()` would take over and wrap the `ChromaticOpticalPSF` inside a `ChromaticObject`.  The `evaluateAtWavelength` of the resulting object (which is now a `ChromaticObject`, similar to how transforming a `Gaussian` say results in a generic `GSObject`) would call `ChromaticOpticalPSF.evaluateAtWavelength()`, transform the result, and return.  This is how `Chromatic`, `ChromaticSum`, etc. all work.  I think this addresses your concern about copying large amounts of code into subclasses of ChromaticObject too.

I agree that it's probably unlikely that anyone will want to transform a `ChromaticOpticalPSF` (or `OpticalPSF`).  However, I don't think we should completely disable a perfectly functional ability.  I'd be more in favor of a warning.

Finally, one thing I should have thought of earlier is mutability, i.e. #511, which probably means that `setupInterpolation` and `noInterpolation` should return new objects instead of operating on themselves.

Hi @ltamaster, first of all I'm very glad this is working yeah :)

Defining destination path is indeed possibile and it depends on how you want to do it. I'll suggest you couple of option starting from what i prefer the most:

### Using proposed `tar -C /`

From manual, tar's `-C` flag:
```
Change to DIR before performing any operations.
This option is order-sensitive, i.e. it affects all options that follow.
```
so if you want to put under `/usr/local/foo/bar/`:
```shell
oggei@cane:/tmp$ cd $(mktemp -d)
oggei@cane:/tmp/tmp.u9nN0XILDP$ mkdir -p usr/local/foo/bar
oggei@cane:/tmp/tmp.u9nN0XILDP$ echo hello > usr/local/foo/bar/my_file
oggei@cane:/tmp/tmp.u9nN0XILDP$ tar cv . > ../package.tar
./
./usr/
./usr/local/
./usr/local/foo/
./usr/local/foo/bar/
./usr/local/foo/bar/my_file
oggei@cane:/tmp/tmp.u9nN0XILDP$
```

That way, you have a `/tmp/package.tar` that when decompressed place a `usr/local/foo/bar/my_file` under your root filesystem. Tar being tar, this is not limited to a single file BTW (in fact even the example above ships 4 dir and 1 file).

A similar concept is used eg. in Debian packaging system, where a filesystem root is produced under `debian/$pkg_name` and then compressed in .deb package.

### Runtime `-C` flag definition

You could write your code so you define operations' root via `-C` flag, and then you tar a hierarchy relative to this root. It's more or less like the above, except you start from a custom root and not / (so you can `tar c file > archive.tar` and then place it where you want via -C).  Seems less straightforward *to me*, but it works.

OK that's all, happy hacking on that :)

```python
trials.add_column('success', 'bool for if the trials was successful')

tirals.add_row({'start': 30, 'end': 40, 'direction': 'L', 'success': 1})
...

nwbfile.trials = trials
```

as opposed to
```python
nwbfile.add_trial({'start': 30.0, 'end': 40.0})
```
since (as far as I can tell) the latter does not allow for additional columns. The first also allows me to specify multiple trial tables, which I think will be necessary in some datasets.


I would prefer kwargs instead of a `dict` for `trials.add_trial`, but that's not a big deal. Would it be difficult to allow for specifying a `dtype` in `trials.add_column`? It's not essential, but would be nice. Also, it would be nice to specify a `default_val`. Also not essential.
